---

[Documentation](/overview)

A **checkpoint** is a snapshot of a coding session attached to a Git commit. Entire.io lets you browse checkpoints by branch, inspect what changed, and trace a commit or PR back to the agent session that produced it.

## 

​

Branch view

Open any repository on entire.io to land on its branch list. Each branch shows recent commits grouped by day, with the checkpoints captured on each commit.

![Checkpoints by branch](https://mintcdn.com/entire/fgG56b7zcMZgFdOf/images/checkpoint-detail-dark.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=fgG56b7zcMZgFdOf&q=85&s=f8cc696d48c8623b660e60a61be165d1)

Each row shows:

  * **Commit SHA** — 7-character prefix
  * **Commit message** — the title of the commit
  * **Agent** — which AI coding agent (or agents) produced the checkpoints on this commit
  * **Code changes** — additions, deletions, and file count
  * **Checkpoint count** — how many checkpoints are attached (shown only when more than zero)
  * **Author** — GitHub avatar of the commit author



## 

​

Checkpoint detail

Click a checkpoint to open its detail view. The view has two main tabs:

  * **Sessions** — every session that contributed to this checkpoint (cross-link to [Inspect a Session](/web/inspect-session))
  * **Changes** — files modified, with a tree on the left and a side-by-side or unified diff on the right

The header above the tabs shows the commit SHA, branch, author, agent, file count, AI percentage, and token usage for the checkpoint.

Use **Alt + ←** and **Alt + →** to step through the previous and next checkpoint on the same branch without leaving the detail view.

## 

​

Linking from a commit or PR

A checkpoint is bound to a commit, so you can reach it from anywhere you can reach the commit.

### 

​

From a commit URL

Entire.io accepts commit SHAs directly:
    
    
    https://entire.io/gh/<org>/<repo>/commit/<commit-sha>
    

This resolves the SHA to its checkpoint and renders the same detail view.

### 

​

From a commit message

When the CLI captures a checkpoint, it appends an `Entire-Checkpoint` trailer to the commit message:
    
    
    fix: handle empty repos in dispatch generation
    
    Entire-Checkpoint: a1b2c3d4e5f6
    

### 

​

From the session view

Inside [Inspect a Session](/web/inspect-session), each session is linked to the checkpoints it produced. Use the checkpoint header’s actions menu to jump out to the underlying commit on GitHub.

## 

​

Rewind to a checkpoint

To restore your local repository to a checkpoint’s state, see [`entire rewind`](/cli/commands#rewind).

## 

​

Troubleshooting

For issues like `Unknown author` on a checkpoint or missing checkpoints, see [Troubleshooting](/web/troubleshooting).

title: Gemini CLI - Entire.io
url: https://docs.entire.io/agents/gemini-cli
description: Gemini CLI is Google's command-line interface for Gemini AI. Entire supports capturing sessions from Gemini CLI, tracking your AI-assisted coding ...
---

[Documentation](/overview)

Gemini CLI is Google’s command-line interface for Gemini AI. Entire supports capturing sessions from Gemini CLI, tracking your AI-assisted coding interactions. The Gemini CLI integration is in preview and may contain bugs.

## 

​

Prerequisites

  * [Entire CLI installed](/cli/installation)
  * [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google/gemini-cli) installed
  * A Git repository with Entire enabled



## 

​

Enable
    
    
    entire agent add gemini
    gemini
    

How it works

Entire integrates with Gemini CLI through Git hooks. When you start Gemini CLI in an Entire-enabled repository:

  1. **Process Detection** — Entire detects when Gemini CLI is running.
  2. **File Monitoring** — file changes are tracked during the session.
  3. **Checkpoint Creation** — created when you or the agent make a Git commit.
  4. **Session Storage** — data saved to the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch.

The session ends when Gemini CLI exits.

What gets captured

Data| Description  
---|---  
File changes| All files modified during the session  
Timestamps| Session and checkpoint timing  
Session boundaries| Start and end of Gemini sessions  
Checkpoints| Rewind points within sessions  
  
Features

**Rewind during sessions.** Use `entire checkpoint rewind` to restore to a previous checkpoint during or after a Gemini CLI session. See the [`rewind` command reference](/cli/commands#rewind) for full flag documentation.**Resume previous sessions.** Switch to a session’s branch with `entire session resume <branch>`. The CLI restores the session log if needed and prints the command to resume the session. See the [`resume` command reference](/cli/commands#resume) for full flag documentation.**Explain commits.** Inspect any commit or checkpoint with `entire checkpoint explain`. See the [`explain` command reference](/cli/commands#explain) for full flag documentation.

Caveats and limitations

  * Conversation transcripts may not be captured depending on Gemini CLI version.
  * Session detection relies on process monitoring rather than dedicated lifecycle hooks.
  * Token usage is not tracked.
  * Works best when you commit at logical stopping points, or tell the agent to handle committing.



Best practices

**Commit at logical points.** Commit when you’ve made meaningful progress:
    
    
    git add .
    git commit -m "Implement feature with Gemini assistance"
    

**Check status regularly.** Monitor your session with `entire status`.

Example workflow
    
    
    # 1. Setup
    cd my-project
    entire agent add gemini
    
    # 2. Start Gemini CLI
    gemini
    
    # 3. Work with Gemini
    # Ask it to write code, make changes, etc.
    
    # 4. Check session (in another terminal)
    entire status
    
    # 5. Rewind if needed
    entire checkpoint rewind
    
    # 6. Commit when ready
    git add .
    git commit -m "Add feature with Gemini"
    
    # 7. Push
    git push
    

## 

​

Troubleshooting

Session not detected

  1. Ensure you’re in an Entire-enabled repo: `entire status`
  2. Verify Gemini CLI is running from within the repository
  3. Check Git hooks are installed: `ls .git/hooks`



No checkpoints created

  1. Checkpoints require file changes
  2. Make sure Gemini is modifying files in the repository
  3. Check your settings in `.entire/settings.json`

payload.text:
**Fixing session review issues**

I need to find a better way to use jq for checking review fields since the current session state doesn't include any. I might consider attaching existing sessions for review, perhaps with something like `entire review attach <id>`, but I'm unsure about findings. There are potential fixes, like cleaning up `.githooks` to avoid duplication of the Entire hooks. I need to remove `--commit` from certain calls to prevent auto commits, which could lead to dirty documents.

**Resolving commit issues**

I’m noticing that the post-commit is creating dirty documents with every relevant commit, which is quite surprising. A minimal fix might be to remove checkpoint calls from hooks completely. However, the user wants the Entire review persisted, so I’ll keep the entire hooks but drop the custom auto checkpoints. Regarding .gitignore, I’m debating whether to track the current untracked hooks or remove and ignore them. It seems the safest immediate fix is to disarm the custom auto-commit lines without leaving any files in limbo.

**Deciding on .githooks**

For durable solutions, I should track the `.githooks/scripts`, but the current hooks were previously untracked. It’s a bit of a messy situation, and I need to clarify the intent before making any edits. The ENTIRE_AUTH_HANDOFF indicates it’s installed and enabled for automation. In the review, there's a decision to make about whether to track these hooks or simply remove and ignore them. It feels like the user is suggesting I make a choice here.

**Investigating Gemini Trust Setup**

I need to handle the Gemini trust and find its configuration. I might start by using `gemini --help` and possibly `gemini mcp`. Setting an environment variable in Entire might be an option, or I could add a `.gemini/settings.json` file. Should I check the trusted folders in the documentation? The user didn’t specifically ask me to browse the web, but the current docs could be available. I'll double-check everything to verify my approach.

payload.message: Root cause so far: the repo made untracked hook files authoritative via `core.hooksPath`, then the hook chain calls both Entire’s generated hook and a pre-existing hook that calls Entire again; the custom checkpoint helper also commits from post-commit/post-merge/post-rewrite. The minimal durable fix is to keep Entire’s generated hooks, remove the custom auto-commit calls from hooks, and stop double-calling Entire in the chained hooks.

content.text: Root cause so far: the repo made untracked hook files authoritative via `core.hooksPath`, then the hook chain calls both Entire’s generated hook and a pre-existing hook that calls Entire again; the custom checkpoint helper also commits from post-commit/post-merge/post-rewrite. The minimal durable fix is to keep Entire’s generated hooks, remove the custom auto-commit calls from hooks, and stop double-calling Entire in the chained hooks.

payload.text:
**Evaluating git commit process**

I need to run some checks on the hooks. I notice that they still append dirty documents after commit or rewrite, which is problematic. It might be undesirable, but it could also minimize issues. Specifically, the "silent bot commits" issue has been addressed, but post-rewrite appending during a rebase could still cause changes to the worktree, which might be disturbing. I wonder if I should just skip the post-rewrite checkpoint entirely. Testing if I can use `git commit --allow-empty` is on the table too.

**Checking syntax and testing environment**

I could run the shell command `sh -n` to check for syntax issues. I'll also grep for `--commit` to count any occurrences in the calls. It seems I might need an environment handshake, but that could be tricky. I want to avoid conducting a broad full review, as that may be unnecessarily complicated. Checking the `.git status` might also provide clarity on the current state. I need to keep things efficient and not overcomplicate.

payload.text:
**Considering closeout packet creation**

I need to decide on the best way to create a closeout packet file. Should I use `apply_patch` or a shell command? Maybe naming it closeout.json is fine, but I need to think about where to save it—possibly in /tmp to avoid having a dirty repo. Using `functions.shell_command` with Python feels like a cleaner approach, but I’m cautious about file creation methods like using cat. I'll explore options to safely create and handle this temporary file.

**Classifying files and managing patches**

I’m considering using `apply_patch` to add a repo file named `.codex-closeout.json`, but that could create a dirty state, which I want to avoid. Instead, I could use `mktemp` with a Python command. However, I’ve been reminded not to use Python for file operations if a simple shell command can do the job. I can create a temporary file in /tmp. 

Next, I need to classify dirty paths like those from .githooks and scripts. I'll categorize them into task-scoped and unrelated files. Lastly, if all task-scoped files are clear, should I commit only those?

**Considering validation and restart**

I’m thinking about the need for validation and whether I should also include a runtime restart. It feels essential to ensure everything works properly before proceeding, but I’m uncertain if the restart is necessary. I want to minimize interruptions and keep the workflow smooth. So, I need to weigh the benefits of restarting against the potential impact on the overall process. Should I validate first, then decide about the restart?

I disarmed the risky Entire hook path without touching the science/scratch lanes.

Changed:
- [post-commit.pre-entire](/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS/.githooks/post-commit.pre-entire): removed duplicate `entire hooks git post-commit` call and changed checkpoint helper from `--auto --commit` to `--auto`.
- [post-merge](/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS/.githooks/post-merge): changed `--auto --commit` to `--auto`.
- [post-rewrite.pre-entire](/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS/.githooks/post-rewrite.pre-entire): removed duplicate `entire hooks git post-rewrite` call and changed `--auto --commit` to `--auto`.
- [entire_checkpoint.py](/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS/scripts/entire_checkpoint.py): import-order cleanup only.
- [ENTIRE_AUTH_HANDOFF.md](/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS/ENTIRE_AUTH_HANDOFF.md): corrected the note so hooks do not claim to auto-commit checkpoints.

Root cause from the pasted `entire review`:
- The repo had `core.hooksPath=.githooks` pointing at untracked active hooks.
- The hook chain double-called Entire for `post-commit` / `post-rewrite`.
- The custom checkpoint helper committed from hooks, including `post-rewrite`, which is the real footgun.
- Gemini synthesis is a separate issue: `GEMINI_CLI_TRUST_WORKSPACE=true` / `--skip-trust` got past trust, but live Gemini then failed with `403 Permission denied on resource project Default Gemini Project`. That needs Gemini auth/project config, not a repo hook patch.
- `entire review --findings` still reports no local findings, consistent with the failed review-session tagging.

Verification:
- `sh -n` passed for all `.githooks/*`.
- `python -m py_compile scripts/entire_checkpoint.py` passed.
- Current `.githooks` contain no `--commit`.
- `agent-closeout-check` passes with `/tmp/flits-closeout.json`.

I did not commit because the repo is still dirty with separate lanes:
- Task-scoped: `.githooks/`, `ENTIRE_AUTH_HANDOFF.md`, `scripts/entire_checkpoint.py`.
- Pre-existing/unrelated: `flits/scattering/__init__.py`, `flits/scattering/broaden.py`, `scattering/scat_analysis/validation_thresholds.py`.
- Follow-up: `scratch/codetection/`.
- Generated/concurrent residue: `docs/entire-tracing-checkpoints.md`.
- A concurrent `_audit_tmp_checkpoint` branch also created `test: probe watched path` and `docs: add Entire tracing checkpoint` while I was working, then returned `main` to `794c717`.

Next clean step: commit only the task-scoped Entire hook/tooling lane after confirming the concurrent audit/test lane is done.
